title: parseInt/parseFloat return a number from garbage input instead of failing
rule: JS parseInt/parseFloat parse the longest valid numeric prefix and silently return it instead of erroring, so garbage produces a plausible-but-wrong number that passes validation: parseInt('12px')===12, parseInt('0x10')===16 (radix auto-detected), parseFloat('1,234.5')===1, parseFloat('1.234,5')===1.234 (a EU decimal comma terminates the scan). For validation use Number(str) (Number('12px') is NaN) plus an explicit isNaN/format check, and always pass an explicit radix to parseInt.
triggers: parseInt returns wrong value for string with units
thousands separator parsed as end of number
European decimal comma gives wrong parseFloat result
